sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disease of the airways characterised by episodic and reversible airflow obstruction, bronchial hyperresponsiveness, and excessive mucus production. Acute exacerbations are marked by a rapid worsening of symptoms due to airway inflammation, smooth muscle contraction (bronchoconstriction), and increased mucus secretion, which together can progress to respiratory failure if untreated. During an asthma attack, the airways become inflamed, oedematous, and narrowed, often in response to specific triggers such as allergens, respiratory infections, or irritants. This pathophysiological process results in symptoms including wheezing, cough, chest tightness, and shortness of breath. In chronic or poorly controlled asthma, persistent inflammation may lead to airway remodelling and irreversible airflow limitation.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Tachypnoea, dyspnoea, wheeze, cyanosis
- Chest Tightness, accessory muscle use, tripod positioning
- Tachycardia, diaphoresis
- Shortening answers/replies to questions i.e., one-word answers

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- If doubt exists as to whether the patient is suffering from asthma or anaphylaxis, treat as anaphylaxis
- Acute asthma is classified into three severity categories:
  - Mild/moderate: patient is able to walk and speak in whole sentences in one breath
  - Severe: unable to speak in complete sentences, visibly breathless, increased work of breathing
  - Life-threatening: drowsy, collapsed, exhausted, cyanotic, poor respiratory effort, altered mental status, bradycardia, or quiet chest
- Pulse oximetry is useful but should not be the only indicator of asthma severity or improvement
- Respiratory symptoms such as dyspnoea and wheezing are non-specific; differential diagnosis should be considered
- Patients presenting with acute asthma may deteriorate rapidly without warning
- If IPPV is required, care should be taken to avoid over-ventilation by allowing a prolonged expiratory phase
- Relievers are best given through a spacer if available, following local dose guidance and repeated assessment

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Position patient appropriately, sitting upright or in a position of comfort
- Conduct Vital Sign Survey, particularly respiratory rate and pulse oximetry
- Consider oxygen therapy and titrate to clinical need
- Administer salbutamol (MDI via spacer or nebuliser) according to local protocol
- If severe/life-threatening presentation, consider adrenaline in line with local protocol and senior consultation

**Ventilate the patient if required:**
  - Extreme care should be taken when ventilating an asthmatic patient; allow adequate exhalation and avoid rapid over-ventilation, which may worsen air trapping, hypotension, and cardiac arrest
  - Officers may assist the patient in the administration of their own medication where clinically indicated
  - Be alert for rapid deterioration

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider ipratropium bromide (nebulised) if indicated
- Consider IM adrenaline for life-threatening asthma in line with local protocol and senior consultation
- Consider magnesium sulphate for severe to life-threatening asthma resistant to first-line treatments in consultation with on-call doctor
- Apply cardiac monitor
- Gain vascular access
- Consider corticosteroid therapy in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider IV fluids to treat hypotension

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
**Differential diagnosis to be considered:**
  - Cardiac Failure
  - Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
  - Foreign body
  - Smoke inhalation
